items.title: Detecting Scleral Icterus as a Marker for Serum Bilirubin Levels
items.content: Scleral icterus is an early and sensitive indicator of hyperbilirubinemia, detectable when serum bilirubin levels reach at least 51 µmol/L (3 mg/dL). This physical examination technique is particularly useful in identifying slight increases in bilirubin due to the high affinity of sclerae for bilirubin.
keyPoints: Scleral icterus indicates serum bilirubin level ≥ 51 µmol/L (3 mg/dL)
Best detected by examining sclerae, particularly under non-fluorescent lighting
Second examination site if needed: underneath the tongue
items.reference: Jaundice · Jaundice

items.title: Differentiating Jaundice from Carotenoderma and Drug-Induced Yellowing
items.content: Distinguishing jaundice from other causes of skin yellowing, such as carotenoderma or drug-induced discoloration, is crucial. In jaundice, the yellow coloration is uniformly distributed over the body and includes the sclerae, whereas in carotenoderma, it spares the sclerae and concentrates on specific areas like palms and soles.
keyPoints: Jaundice: uniform skin yellowing including sclerae
Carotenoderma: yellowing of palms, soles, forehead, nasolabial folds; sparing of sclerae
Drug-induced yellowing (quinacrine, sunitinib, sorafenib) affects 4–37% of patients
items.reference: Jaundice · Jaundice

items.title: Understanding Bilirubin Production and Transport
items.content: Bilirubin is primarily produced from the breakdown of hemoglobin in senescent red blood cells, accounting for 80–85% of daily production. It is formed in reticuloendothelial cells through enzymatic reactions involving heme oxygenase and biliverdin reductase. Unconjugated bilirubin binds to albumin for transport to the liver.
keyPoints: Bilirubin is a breakdown product of hemoglobin, accounting for 80–85% of daily production (4 mg/kg body weight).
Formation occurs in reticuloendothelial cells via heme oxygenase and biliverdin reductase.
Unconjugated bilirubin binds to albumin for transport to the liver.
items.reference: Jaundice · PRODUCTION AND METABOLISM OF BILIRUBIN

items.title: Mechanism of Bilirubin Conjugation and Excretion
items.content: In hepatocytes, unconjugated bilirubin is conjugated with glucuronic acid by UDPGT, making it water-soluble. Conjugated bilirubin is excreted into bile via MRP2 and reabsorbed in the sinusoids by OATP1B1/3. In the intestine, bacterial enzymes convert it to urobilinogen, with most being excreted in feces.
keyPoints: Conjugation of bilirubin occurs via UDPGT in hepatocytes.
Conjugated bilirubin is transported into bile by MRP2 and reabsorbed by OATP1B1/3.
In the intestine, conjugated bilirubin forms urobilinogen, with 80–90% excreted in feces.
items.reference: Jaundice · PRODUCTION AND METABOLISM OF BILIRUBIN

items.title: Pathways and Clinical Implications of Bilirubin Metabolism
items.content: Bilirubin metabolism involves its conversion to urobilinogen in the gut, with 10–20% undergoing enterohepatic recycling. Increased urinary excretion of urobilinogen may indicate increased bilirubin production or decreased hepatic clearance.
keyPoints: Urobilinogen is formed from unconjugated bilirubin in the gut, with 10–20% undergoing enterohepatic cycling.
Increased urinary excretion of urobilinogen can indicate increased bilirubin production or decreased hepatic clearance.
items.reference: Jaundice · PRODUCTION AND METABOLISM OF BILIRUBIN

items.title: Understanding Serum Bilirubin Fractions and Their Clinical Implications
items.content: The measurement of serum bilirubin involves distinguishing between direct (conjugated) and indirect (unconjugated) fractions using the van den Bergh reaction. The direct fraction, reacting without an accelerator, approximates conjugated bilirubin levels, while the total bilirubin includes both fractions after adding alcohol to release unconjugated bilirubin from albumin. Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia is indicated when the direct fraction is less than 15% of the total serum bilirubin.
keyPoints: Normal serum bilirubin concentration: 17-26 µmol/L (1-1.5 mg/dL).
Total serum bilirubin in 95% of normal population: 3.4-15.4 µmol/L (0.2-0.9 mg/dL).
Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia when direct fraction <15% of total.
Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia suggests hepatobiliary pathology and is associated with bilirubinuria, except in delta bilirubin cases.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF SERUM BILIRUBIN

items.title: Delta Bilirubin's Role in Jaundice and Hepatobiliary Disorders
items.content: In patients with cholestasis or hepatobiliary disorders, delta bilirubin (albumin-linked conjugated bilirubin) forms when hepatic excretion of bilirubin glucuronides is impaired. This fraction has a prolonged half-life due to its binding to albumin, explaining why some jaundiced patients with liver disease do not exhibit bilirubinuria during recovery and why serum bilirubin levels decline slowly.
keyPoints: Delta bilirubin forms when hepatic excretion of bilirubin glucuronides is impaired.
Albumin-linked conjugated bilirubin has a half-life similar to albumin (18-20 days).
Delta bilirubin explains lack of bilirubinuria and slow decline in serum bilirubin levels during recovery.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF SERUM BILIRUBIN

items.title: Advantages of Alternative Techniques for Bilirubin Measurement
items.content: Alternative techniques to the van den Bergh method provide a more accurate understanding of bilirubin metabolism. They reveal that in normal individuals or those with Gilbert's syndrome, nearly all serum bilirubin is unconjugated, and in jaundiced patients with hepatobiliary disease, total serum bilirubin concentrations are lower than diazo methods suggest due to the presence of other diazo-positive compounds.
keyPoints: Almost 100% of serum bilirubin is unconjugated in normal individuals or those with Gilbert's syndrome.
<3% of serum bilirubin is monoconjugated in these groups.
Total serum bilirubin concentrations are lower than diazo methods suggest in jaundiced patients with hepatobiliary disease due to other diazo-positive compounds.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF SERUM BILIRUBIN

items.title: Bilirubinuria Indicates Hepatobiliary Disease
items.content: The presence of bilirubin in urine signifies conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, indicating a hepatobiliary disorder. This is because unconjugated bilirubin cannot be filtered by the kidneys due to its binding with albumin.
keyPoints: Only conjugated bilirubin appears in urine as it can be filtered at the glomerulus and not reabsorbed completely by proximal tubules.
Bilirubinuria on a urine dipstick test (Ictotest) suggests elevated conjugated bilirubin that is not excreted from the liver, implying hepatobiliary disease.
False-negative results can occur in prolonged cholestasis due to delta bilirubin's covalent binding with albumin.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF URINE BILIRUBIN | Jaundice

items.title: Differentiating Hyperbilirubinemia Types via Urine Tests
items.content: Urine tests help differentiate between unconjugated and conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Conjugated bilirubin presence in urine indicates impaired excretion or regurgitation, while its absence suggests issues with uptake or conjugation.
keyPoints: Unconjugated bilirubin is not found in urine due to its binding with albumin and lack of filtration by the kidneys.
Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia can be inferred from bilirubinuria, aiding in distinguishing between hepatocellular or cholestatic liver disorders.
Interpretation of urine tests should consider potential false negatives in cases of prolonged cholestasis.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF URINE BILIRUBIN | Jaundice

items.subtopic: ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.title: Hemolytic Disorders and Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.content: In cases of isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, it's crucial to determine if the cause is hemolysis or impaired hepatic uptake/conjugation. Hemolytic disorders can be inherited (e.g., spherocytosis) or acquired (e.g., microangiopathic hemolytic anemia). Serum bilirubin levels rarely exceed 86 μmol/L in these conditions unless there's coexistent dysfunction.
keyPoints: Determine if hyperbilirubinemia is due to hemolysis or impaired hepatic uptake/conjugation.
Inherited hemolytic disorders include spherocytosis and sickle cell anemia.
Serum bilirubin levels rarely exceed 86 μmol/L (5 mg/dL) in isolated hemolytic conditions.
items.reference: Jaundice · ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia

items.subtopic: ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.title: Crigler-Najjar Syndrome and Bilirubin Conjugation Defects
items.content: Crigler-Najjar syndrome types I and II are genetic conditions characterized by impaired bilirubin conjugation. Type I is severe with bilirubin >342 μmol/L, often leading to kernicterus and death in infancy. Type II patients have serum levels of 103–428 µmol/L and may survive into adulthood.
keyPoints: Crigler-Najjar type I: Severe jaundice (>342 μmol/L), risk of kernicterus, often fatal in early life.
Crigler-Najjar type II: Serum bilirubin 103–428 µmol/L, reduced enzyme activity, survival into adulthood possible.
items.reference: Jaundice · ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia

items.subtopic: ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.title: Gilbert's Syndrome: Mild and Common Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.content: Gilbert's syndrome is a common condition with mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia due to reduced bilirubin UDPGT activity. Serum levels are usually <103 μmol/L, fluctuating during stress or illness. It affects 3–7% of the population and is generally benign.
keyPoints: Gilbert's syndrome: Mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (<103 μmol/L).
Occurs in 3–7% of the population, more common in males.
Generally benign with no significant clinical consequences.
items.reference: Jaundice · ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.title: Differentiating Hepatocellular from Cholestatic Processes
items.content: The pattern of liver enzyme abnormalities helps differentiate between hepatocellular and cholestatic processes. In hepatocellular disease, aminotransferases (ALT, AST) are disproportionately elevated compared to alkaline phosphatase (ALP). Conversely, in cholestasis, ALP is disproportionately elevated relative to aminotransferases.
keyPoints: Hepatocellular process: Elevated ALT and AST disproportionate to ALP
Cholestatic process: Elevated ALP disproportionate to ALT and AST
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.title: Assessing Liver Function with Albumin and Prothrombin Time
items.content: Albumin levels and prothrombin time are critical for assessing liver function. A low albumin level suggests chronic liver disease such as cirrhosis or cancer, while a normal level indicates an acute process like viral hepatitis. An elevated prothrombin time can indicate vitamin K deficiency due to malabsorption or significant hepatocellular dysfunction.
keyPoints: Low albumin: Suggests chronic processes (cirrhosis, cancer)
Normal albumin: Suggests acute processes (viral hepatitis, choledocholithiasis)
Elevated prothrombin time: Indicates vitamin K deficiency or hepatocellular dysfunction
Prothrombin time not correcting with vitamin K suggests severe hepatocellular injury
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.title: Clinical History and Physical Examination in Jaundice Evaluation
items.content: A thorough medical history is crucial for evaluating jaundice, focusing on potential exposures to hepatotoxins, recent travel, and symptoms like arthralgias or myalgias. Physical examination should assess nutritional status, signs of chronic liver disease, and abdominal findings such as splenomegaly or ascites.
keyPoints: History: Includes chemical exposure, travel, jaundice in contacts, and symptom onset
Arthralgias/myalgias predating jaundice suggest hepatitis
Physical exam: Assess for stigmata of chronic liver disease (e.g., spider nevi, gynecomastia)
Abdominal findings: Splenomegaly or ascites may indicate cirrhosis or malignancy
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)
items.title: Differentiating Hepatocellular Conditions
items.content: Hepatocellular diseases causing jaundice can be differentiated by specific patterns in aminotransferase levels. Alcoholic hepatitis is characterized by an AST-to-ALT ratio of at least 2:1, with AST rarely exceeding 300 U/L. In contrast, acute viral and toxin-related hepatitis typically present with aminotransferase levels >500 U/L, where ALT is greater than or equal to AST.
keyPoints: Alcoholic hepatitis: AST-to-ALT ratio ≥2:1, AST <300 U/L
Acute viral/toxin-related hepatitis: Aminotransferases >500 U/L, ALT ≥AST
Values 25 times normal or higher indicate acute hepatocellular diseases
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)
items.title: Testing for Hepatocellular Diseases
items.content: When hepatocellular disease is suspected, testing should include assays for hepatitis A IgM antibody, hepatitis B surface antigen and core IgM antibody, hepatitis C viral RNA, and possibly hepatitis E IgM antibody. Ceruloplasmin is the initial screening test for Wilson's disease, while autoimmune hepatitis is tested with antinuclear antibody, anti-smooth muscle antibody assays, and specific immunoglobulin measurements.
keyPoints: Hepatitis A: Hepatitis A IgM antibody assay
Hepatitis B: Surface antigen and core IgM antibody assay
Hepatitis C: Viral RNA test (antibody may take up to 6 weeks)
Wilson's disease: Ceruloplasmin as initial screening
Autoimmune hepatitis: Antinuclear, anti-smooth muscle antibodies, specific immunoglobulins
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)
items.title: Approach to Cholestatic Conditions
items.content: To differentiate intrahepatic from extrahepatic cholestasis, an ultrasound is the initial test. It detects biliary tree dilation with high sensitivity and specificity. Absence of dilation suggests intrahepatic cholestasis; presence indicates extrahepatic cholestasis. Further imaging like CT or MRCP may be needed to identify obstruction sites.
keyPoints: Ultrasound: First step for cholestatic disorders
Intrahepatic cholestasis: No biliary dilation on ultrasound
Extrahepatic cholestasis: Biliary dilation present
Further imaging (CT, MRCP) may be needed to identify obstruction
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.title: Recognizing Drug-Induced Cholestasis and Its Management
items.content: Intrahepatic cholestasis can be induced by various drugs, with anabolic and contraceptive steroids being the most common culprits. While typically reversible upon cessation of the offending agent, recovery may take months. Chronic cases may involve progressive fibrosis despite early discontinuation, particularly with chlorpromazine and prochlorperazine.
keyPoints: Anabolic and contraceptive steroids are commonly associated with cholestasis.
Cholestatic hepatitis has been reported with drugs like chlorpromazine, erythromycin estolate, and others.
Discontinuation of the offending drug usually reverses cholestasis, but resolution may take months; chronic cases can progress despite early discontinuation.
items.reference: Jaundice · 1. Intrahepatic

items.title: Diagnosing Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC) and Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC)
items.content: Primary biliary cholangitis is an autoimmune disease predominantly affecting women, characterized by the destruction of interlobular bile ducts. Diagnosis involves detecting antimitochondrial antibodies present in 95% of patients. PSC involves fibrosis and destruction of larger bile ducts, diagnosed via MRCP showing segmental strictures. About 75% of PSC patients also have inflammatory bowel disease.
keyPoints: PBC is an autoimmune condition with antimitochondrial antibodies found in 95% of cases.
PSC diagnosis involves cholangiography (MRCP) demonstrating segmental strictures.
Approximately 75% of PSC patients also have inflammatory bowel disease.
items.reference: Jaundice · 1. Intrahepatic

items.title: Understanding Familial Forms of Intrahepatic Cholestasis: PFIC and BRIC
items.content: Familial intrahepatic cholestatic syndromes include progressive familial intrahepatic cholestasis (PFIC) types 1–3 and benign recurrent intrahepatic cholestasis (BRIC) types 1 and 2. BRIC presents with episodic pruritus, cholestasis, and jaundice without leading to chronic liver disease; serum bile acids are elevated during episodes but $ \\$gamma $-GT remains normal. PFIC begins in childhood, is progressive, and involves high serum bile acid levels; type 3 PFIC uniquely shows elevated $ \\$gamma $-GT.
keyPoints: BRIC is characterized by episodic pruritus, cholestasis, jaundice, with elevated serum bile acids but normal $ \\$gamma $-GT.
PFIC disorders are progressive from childhood and involve high serum bile acid levels; type 3 PFIC also shows high $ \\$gamma $-GT.
Familial intrahepatic cholestasis includes PFIC types 1–3 and BRIC types 1 and 2.
items.reference: Jaundice · 1. Intrahepatic

items.question: Which site is recommended for examining if scleral icterus is suspected but difficult to detect?
items.explanation: If scleral icterus is suspected, examining underneath the tongue is recommended.
items.reference: Jaundice · Jaundice

items.question: What serum bilirubin level does the presence of scleral icterus indicate?
items.explanation: Scleral icterus indicates a serum bilirubin level of at least 51 $ \mu $mol/L (3 mg/dL).
items.reference: Jaundice · Jaundice

items.question: Which condition is characterized by yellow discoloration sparing the sclerae?
items.explanation: In carotenoderma, the yellow discoloration spares the sclerae.
items.reference: Jaundice · Jaundice

items.question: What is the primary source of bilirubin production in the body?
options: Breakdown of hemoglobin in senescent red blood cells
Premature destruction of erythroid cells in bone marrow
items.explanation: About 80–85% of bilirubin is derived from the breakdown of hemoglobin in senescent red blood cells.
items.reference: Jaundice · PRODUCTION AND METABOLISM OF BILIRUBIN

items.question: Which enzyme catalyzes the conversion of heme to biliverdin?
items.explanation: The enzyme heme oxygenase catalyzes the conversion of heme to biliverdin.
items.reference: Jaundice · PRODUCTION AND METABOLISM OF BILIRUBIN

items.question: How is unconjugated bilirubin transported in the bloodstream?
items.explanation: Unconjugated bilirubin is transported in the bloodstream by binding noncovalently to albumin.
items.reference: Jaundice · PRODUCTION AND METABOLISM OF BILIRUBIN

items.question: Which enzyme catalyzes the conjugation of glucuronic acid to bilirubin?
items.explanation: The enzyme bilirubin uridine diphosphate-glucuronosyl transferase (UDPGT) catalyzes the conjugation of glucuronic acid to bilirubin.
items.reference: Jaundice · PRODUCTION AND METABOLISM OF BILIRUBIN

items.question: Which substance is used to split bilirubin into dipyrrylmethene azopigments in the van den Bergh reaction?
items.explanation: Bilirubin is exposed to diazotized sulfanilic acid in the van den Bergh reaction.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF SERUM BILIRUBIN

items.question: What does the direct fraction of bilirubin represent?
items.explanation: The direct fraction provides an approximation of the conjugated bilirubin level in serum.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF SERUM BILIRUBIN

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of delta bilirubin?
options: It is not filtered by renal glomeruli
It reacts quickly with diazo reagents
items.explanation: Delta bilirubin, covalently linked to albumin, is not filtered by the renal glomeruli.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF SERUM BILIRUBIN

items.question: Why might serum bilirubin levels decline more slowly than expected in some recovering jaundiced patients?
options: Due to rapid clearance of unconjugated bilirubin
Because of the presence of delta bilirubin
As a result of increased production of bilirubin
Due to decreased conjugation by liver cells
items.explanation: The prolonged half-life of albumin-bound conjugated bilirubin (delta bilirubin) accounts for slower decline in serum levels.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF SERUM BILIRUBIN

items.question: Which type of bilirubin is found in urine when present?
options: None, as bilirubin is not excreted in the urine
items.explanation: Only conjugated bilirubin is filtered by the kidney and can be found in the urine.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF URINE BILIRUBIN | Jaundice

items.question: What does a positive bilirubinuria on a urine dipstick test indicate?
options: Elevation of conjugated bilirubin fraction that cannot be excreted from the liver
items.explanation: A positive test indicates an elevation of the conjugated bilirubin fraction that cannot be excreted, suggesting hepatobiliary disease.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF URINE BILIRUBIN | Jaundice

items.question: Why might a urine dipstick test for bilirubin yield a false-negative result in patients with prolonged cholestasis?
options: Unconjugated bilirubin is not filtered by the kidneys
Delta bilirubin, which is covalently bound to albumin, is not filtered by the renal glomeruli
Conjugated bilirubin is completely reabsorbed in the proximal tubules
The test lacks sensitivity for detecting low levels of conjugated bilirubin
items.explanation: Delta bilirubin, which predominates in prolonged cholestasis and is bound to albumin, is not filtered by the kidneys.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF URINE BILIRUBIN | Jaundice

items.subtopic: ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.question: Which condition is characterized by a complete absence of bilirubin UDPGT activity, leading to severe jaundice and neurologic impairment?
items.explanation: Crigler-Najjar type I is characterized by a complete absence of bilirubin UDPGT activity, leading to severe jaundice and neurologic impairment.
items.reference: Jaundice · ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia

items.subtopic: ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.question: Which syndrome is associated with mutations in the gene for MRP2, leading to altered excretion of bilirubin into the bile ducts?
items.explanation: Dubin-Johnson syndrome involves mutations in the gene for MRP2, leading to altered excretion of bilirubin into the bile ducts.
items.reference: Jaundice · ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia

items.subtopic: ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.question: Which condition is marked by a reduction in bilirubin UDPGT activity to typically ≤10% and can be treated with phenobarbital?
items.explanation: Crigler-Najjar type II is marked by a reduction in bilirubin UDPGT activity to typically ≤10% and can be treated with phenobarbital.
items.reference: Jaundice · ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia

items.subtopic: ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.question: Which condition is most common, affecting 3–7% of the population and characterized by mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia?
items.explanation: Gilbert's syndrome is the most common condition, affecting 3–7% of the population and characterized by mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
items.reference: Jaundice · ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.question: Which laboratory test result is most helpful in distinguishing between a hepatocellular process and a cholestatic process?
items.explanation: Enzyme tests help differentiate between hepatocellular and cholestatic processes by comparing the rise in aminotransferases to alkaline phosphatase.
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.question: What does an elevated prothrombin time indicate in jaundiced patients?
options: Vitamin K deficiency or significant hepatocellular dysfunction
items.explanation: An elevated prothrombin time indicates vitamin K deficiency due to prolonged jaundice and malabsorption of vitamin K or significant hepatocellular dysfunction.
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.question: What does a low serum albumin level suggest in jaundiced patients?
options: Chronic liver disease such as cirrhosis or cancer
items.explanation: A low serum albumin level suggests a chronic process like cirrhosis or cancer.
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.question: Which physical examination finding is suggestive of advanced cirrhosis?
options: Severe right-upper-quadrant tenderness with respiratory arrest on inspiration
items.explanation: Right pleural effusion, even in the absence of clinically apparent ascites, may be seen in advanced cirrhosis.
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)
items.question: Which pattern of aminotransferase levels is typically seen in patients with alcoholic hepatitis?
options: AST-to-ALT ratio of at least 2:1, AST level rarely exceeds 300 U/L
ALT greater than or equal to AST, levels >500 U/L
items.explanation: Alcoholic hepatitis is characterized by an AST-to-ALT ratio of at least 2:1, and the AST level rarely exceeds 300 U/L.

items.question: What is the initial screening test for Wilson's disease?
items.explanation: The initial screening test for Wilson's disease is ceruloplasmin.

items.explanation: ERCP is the 'gold standard' for identifying choledocholithiasis.

items.explanation: Both hepatitis B and C viruses can cause cholestatic hepatitis (fibrosing cholestatic hepatitis), which is a variant of the disease.

items.statement: Darkening of urine due to bilirubinuria indicates an elevation in the indirect serum bilirubin fraction.
items.explanation: Bilirubinuria indicates an elevation of the direct (conjugated) serum bilirubin fraction.
items.reference: Jaundice · Jaundice

items.statement: The yellow coloration in jaundice is concentrated on the palms, soles, forehead, and nasolabial folds.
items.explanation: In jaundice, the yellow coloration of the skin is uniformly distributed over the body.
items.reference: Jaundice · Jaundice

items.statement: Conjugated bilirubin is reabsorbed by the intestinal mucosa.
items.explanation: Conjugated bilirubin is not reabsorbed due to its hydrophilicity and increased molecular size.
items.reference: Jaundice · PRODUCTION AND METABOLISM OF BILIRUBIN

items.statement: Bilirubin conjugation occurs in the cytosol of hepatocytes.
items.explanation: Bilirubin conjugation with glucuronic acid occurs in the endoplasmic reticulum, not the cytosol.
items.reference: Jaundice · PRODUCTION AND METABOLISM OF BILIRUBIN

items.statement: Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia is present when the direct fraction is greater than 15% of the total serum bilirubin.
items.explanation: Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia is present when the direct fraction is less than 15%.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF SERUM BILIRUBIN

items.statement: The normal serum bilirubin concentration usually ranges between 17 and 26 µmol/L.
items.explanation: Normal serum bilirubin concentrations are indeed between 17 and 26 µmol/L.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF SERUM BILIRUBIN

items.statement: Unconjugated bilirubin can be detected in urine.
items.explanation: Unconjugated bilirubin is bound to albumin and not filtered by the kidneys, so it cannot be found in urine.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF URINE BILIRUBIN | Jaundice

items.statement: The presence of conjugated bilirubin in urine always indicates normal liver function.
items.explanation: Conjugated bilirubin in the urine suggests hepatobiliary disease, as it implies an inability to excrete bilirubin from the liver.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF URINE BILIRUBIN | Jaundice

items.subtopic: ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.statement: Crigler-Najjar type I patients can survive into adulthood with appropriate management.
items.explanation: Crigler-Najjar type I is a severe condition often leading to death in infancy or childhood due to kernicterus.
items.reference: Jaundice · ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia

items.subtopic: ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.statement: Patients with Dubin-Johnson syndrome present with asymptomatic jaundice.
items.explanation: Dubin-Johnson syndrome is characterized by asymptomatic jaundice due to altered bilirubin excretion.
items.reference: Jaundice · ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.statement: Total serum bilirubin measurement is the most helpful test in differentiating between hepatocellular and cholestatic processes.
items.explanation: Serum bilirubin can be elevated in both conditions, so enzyme tests are more useful for differentiation.
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.statement: A history of arthralgias and myalgias predating jaundice suggests hepatitis.
items.explanation: Arthralgias and myalgias before jaundice suggest viral or drug-related hepatitis.
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)
items.statement: Hepatitis C antibody can become detectable within 1 week after exposure.
items.explanation: The hepatitis C antibody can take up to 6 weeks to become detectable, making it unreliable for acute hepatitis C.
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)
items.statement: Ultrasound is the initial diagnostic test of choice for intrahepatic cholestasis.
items.explanation: Ultrasound can detect dilation of the biliary tree and suggest intrahepatic cholestasis when there's no dilation.
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.statement: Cholestasis of pregnancy typically resolves after delivery.
items.explanation: Cholestasis of pregnancy occurs in the second and third trimesters and resolves post-delivery.
items.reference: Jaundice · 1. Intrahepatic

items.statement: Ischemic hepatitis is characterized by a gradual peak in serum bilirubin.
items.explanation: Ischemic hepatitis shows an acute and dramatic elevation in serum aminotransferases followed by a gradual peak in serum bilirubin.
items.reference: Jaundice · 1. Intrahepatic

items.assertion: Bilirubin is made water-soluble by conjugation with glucuronic acid.
items.reason: This process disrupts the hydrophobic internal hydrogen bonds in bilirubin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Jaundice · PRODUCTION AND METABOLISM OF BILIRUBIN

items.assertion: Unconjugated bilirubin is excreted in urine.
items.reason: A small fraction of urobilinogen escapes hepatic uptake and is filtered across the renal glomerulus.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but unconjugated bilirubin itself is not directly excreted in urine; rather, it's the urobilinogen derived from bilirubin.
items.reference: Jaundice · PRODUCTION AND METABOLISM OF BILIRUBIN

items.assertion: Detection of bilirubin in urine via dipstick test is helpful to confirm the presence of conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
items.reason: Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia is always associated with bilirubinuria, except when delta bilirubin is present.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why urine dipstick tests are useful for confirming conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF SERUM BILIRUBIN

items.assertion: Total serum bilirubin concentrations measured by more accurate methods are higher than those found with diazo methods in jaundiced patients.
items.reason: There are diazo-positive compounds distinct from bilirubin present in the serum of these patients.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; total serum bilirubin concentrations measured by more accurate methods are lower than those found with diazo methods.
items.reference: Jaundice · MEASUREMENT OF SERUM BILIRUBIN

items.subtopic: ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.assertion: Gilbert's syndrome is associated with mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
items.reason: Gilbert's syndrome involves reduced bilirubin UDPGT activity (typically 10–35% of normal).
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Jaundice · ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia

items.subtopic: ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia
items.assertion: Rotor syndrome is caused by a deficiency in bilirubin UDPGT activity.
items.reason: Rotor syndrome results from deficiencies in the hepatic drug reuptake transporters OATP1B1 and OATP1B3.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Rotor syndrome is not caused by a deficiency in bilirubin UDPGT activity.
items.reference: Jaundice · ISOLATED ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN | TABLE 52-1 Causes of Isolated Hyperbilirubinemia

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.assertion: An enlarged tender liver could signify an acutely congested liver secondary to right-sided heart failure.
items.reason: An enlarged tender liver is more commonly associated with viral or alcoholic hepatitis, or infiltrative processes like amyloidosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is true but the reason provided does not correctly explain it.
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.assertion: A normal albumin level in jaundiced patients suggests a chronic process such as cirrhosis.
items.reason: A normal albumin level is suggestive of an acute process like viral hepatitis or choledocholithiasis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; a normal albumin level indicates an acute process, not chronic.
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)
items.assertion: Autoimmune hepatitis is typically seen in young to middle-aged women.
items.reason: Autoimmune diseases are more prevalent in females due to hormonal and genetic factors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why autoimmune hepatitis is more common in this demographic.
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)
items.assertion: ERCP is used primarily for diagnostic purposes.
items.reason: ERCP allows therapeutic interventions, including the removal of common bile duct stones and placement of stents.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because ERCP is not only diagnostic but also therapeutic.
items.reference: Jaundice · ELEVATION OF SERUM BILIRUBIN WITH OTHER LIVER TEST ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.assertion: Vanishing bile duct syndrome can occur idiopathically.
items.reason: Chlorpromazine is associated with vanishing bile duct syndrome.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but chlorpromazine association does not explain the idiopathic occurrence.
items.reference: Jaundice · 1. Intrahepatic

items.assertion: Primary biliary cholangitis predominantly affects women.
items.reason: It is characterized by progressive destruction of interlobular bile ducts.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why it predominantly affects women.
items.reference: Jaundice · 1. Intrahepatic
